messages.content:
You are a GDPR compliance analyst. When given a document excerpt, evaluate it step by step against each GDPR requirement. For each step, quote the relevant text if found, or state it is missing. End with a structured verdict listing all violations. Always follow this exact format:

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): ...
Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): ...
Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): ...
Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): ...
Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): ...
Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): ...
Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): ...
Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): ...
Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): ...
Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): ...

VERDICT: [Compliant / Non-Compliant / Partially Compliant]
VIOLATIONS: [list each article breached]
RECOMMENDATIONS: [one line per fix needed]

Assess the following employee monitoring policy for GDPR compliance:

"Employees should be aware that company devices including laptops and phones are subject to monitoring. We may review emails, internet usage, and application activity. This is necessary for security and productivity purposes. Monitoring data is retained for 12 months. Employees may submit a written request to HR to access records held about them."

Step 1 — Lawful basis (Art. 6): [PARTIAL] "Security and productivity" suggests legitimate interest (Art. 6(1)(f)), but a legitimate interest assessment (LIA) is not referenced. Employee monitoring requires demonstrating the interest is not overridden by employee rights — this is not shown.

Step 2 — Special category data (Art. 9): [PARTIAL] Email and application monitoring could inadvertently capture health, religious, or union data. No safeguards for special category data are mentioned.

Step 3 — Purpose limitation (Art. 5): [PARTIAL] Two purposes stated (security and productivity) but not separately defined. These are distinct purposes requiring separate justification; bundling them is insufficient.

Step 4 — Data minimization (Art. 5): [FAIL] Potentially excessive. Monitoring all emails and all application activity is broad. No mention of targeted or proportionate monitoring.

Step 5 — Retention period (Art. 5): [PASS] "12 months" is a defined retention period.

Step 6 — Data subject rights (Art. 15-21): [PARTIAL] Right to access is mentioned via written HR request. Rights to rectification (Art. 16), erasure (Art. 17), restriction (Art. 18), and objection (Art. 21) are absent.

Step 7 — Third-party disclosure (Art. 28): [FAIL] Not addressed. If third-party security tools or cloud platforms process monitoring data, these must be disclosed.

Step 8 — International transfers (Art. 46): [FAIL] Not addressed. Security and monitoring tools often involve non-EEA cloud providers — not disclosed.

Step 9 — DPO appointment (Art. 37): [FAIL] Not mentioned. Large-scale systematic employee monitoring likely triggers mandatory DPO appointment under Art. 37(1)(b).

Step 10 — Breach notification (Art. 33): [FAIL] Not addressed in this policy.

VERDICT: Non-Compliant
VIOLATIONS: Art. 6 (LIA not documented), Art. 9 (special category risk unmitigated), Art. 5 (purposes not separated, minimization concern), Art. 15-21 (incomplete rights), Art. 28 (processor disclosure missing), Art. 37 (DPO likely required)
RECOMMENDATIONS:
- Conduct and document a Legitimate Interest Assessment for each monitoring purpose
- Separate security monitoring and productivity monitoring as distinct processing activities
- Define scope limits — specify what triggers monitoring rather than blanket surveillance
- Add safeguards for incidental capture of special category data
- List all employee rights with clear exercise instructions
- Disclose third-party monitoring tool providers
- Assess DPO requirement and appoint if applicable
